explicit_text: I entered the Vietnam War in 1970 as part of the United States Air Force. My role was mainly supporting the war effort by repairing aircraft, especially the C-130 Hercules, which were crucial for cargo missions. I was stationed in places like Taiwan and Thailand, and sometimes flew into combat zones near Vietnam and Cambodia. The war was intense, and I saw firsthand the toll it took on friends like Danny Bowling, who was severely wounded in combat.

explicit_text: I chose to join the United States Air Force in 1970 to avoid being drafted into the Army and sent directly to the front lines. I worked as an aircraft structural repair technician, mainly on C-130 cargo planes, and was stationed at bases like England Air Force Base in Louisiana and overseas in Taiwan and Thailand. The Air Force offered me valuable training and a lifetime career skill, and I was proud to earn the Air Force Commendation Medal for my work. Although the Air Force personnel were not always fully supported by the public during the Vietnam War, I felt my service was important.

explicit_text: My first duty station after basic and technical training was England Air Force Base in Louisiana. It was there that I worked under an experienced shop chief and began honing my skills as a sheet metal mechanic on aircraft. This base was an important starting point for me before I was deployed overseas to support the Vietnam War. England Air Force Base gave me the foundation I needed to succeed in my Air Force career.

explicit_text: After my initial assignment in Louisiana, I was stationed in Taiwan as part of the 374th Tactical Airlift Wing. Taiwan was a key location for supporting the Vietnam War, and from there we maintained and repaired C-130 cargo planes that flew missions into Southeast Asia. The Taiwanese people were very trustworthy and industrious, and my experience there was positive despite the challenges of the war. Taiwan was an important part of my overseas service during that period.

explicit_text: I was assigned to the 374th Tactical Airlift Wing while stationed in Taiwan during the Vietnam War. Our main mission was to support combat operations by maintaining and repairing C-130 Hercules cargo aircraft. We had a large team of mechanics and supervisors working around the clock to keep between 80 to 120 aircraft operational. The work was demanding but crucial to the war effort, and I even led a shift crew at Korat Royal Thai Air Force Base despite being a relatively junior airman.

explicit_text: The C-130 Hercules was the aircraft I primarily worked on as an aircraft structural repair technician during the Vietnam War. These cargo planes were vital for transporting supplies and troops, often flying into dangerous forward operating locations in Thailand and Vietnam. I repaired damage from combat landings and small arms fire, sometimes under intense pressure to get the planes back in the air quickly. Working on the C-130 gave me valuable skills and a sense of contributing directly to the war effort.

explicit_text: I was stationed in Thailand for temporary duty assignments and worked at bases like Utapao Royal Thai Navy Airfield and Korat Royal Thai Air Force Base. Thailand was a hub for forward operating locations where C-130 aircraft flew missions into Vietnam and Cambodia. The bases were busy and demanding, with long work hours and a lively off-duty scene in the local bars. The Thai people I encountered were generally polite, though the areas frequented by GIs were mostly commercialized for soldiers' spending.

explicit_text: Vietnam was the central focus of the war I supported while in the Air Force. Although I was mostly stationed outside Vietnam, I occasionally flew into combat zones near Vietnam to repair aircraft damaged during missions. The war was intense and dangerous, and I saw the effects on friends and fellow servicemen who were wounded or psychologically scarred. Vietnam was the backdrop for much of my military service during the early 1970s.

explicit_text: During my service, I was once sent on a mission to Cambodia to repair a C-130 that had blown a tire and damaged its structure on landing. At that time, Cambodia was an active combat zone with rebel forces laying siege to Phnom Penh. We supported the Cambodian government by flying supply missions from bases like Utapao in Thailand. The situation was tense and scary, as the war was very close, but I mostly focused on the technical work rather than the politics.

explicit_text: Danny Bowling was a close friend of mine from high school who was drafted into the Army before I joined the Air Force. He served in Vietnam and was severely wounded, losing his left hand and suffering deep psychological scars. He came back within a year but was never the same, struggling with alcoholism and trauma. His experience was a stark reminder of the harsh realities faced by combat troops during the Vietnam War.

explicit_text: My father was a veteran of World War II and served in France during that conflict. He advised me to join the Air Force instead of risking being drafted into the Army and sent to the front lines, based on his own experiences. His perspective influenced my decision to seek a role that kept me away from direct combat. World War II shaped my family's military history and my approach to service.

explicit_text: During my time in the Air Force, I encountered Marines who were often very intense and angry, especially those coming from the front lines. They were known for being wild and wanting to fight, reflecting the harsh combat experiences they endured. While I didn't serve in the Marines, I saw the psychological toll the war took on them and how different their experience was compared to mine in the Air Force. The Marines represented the fierce combat element of the Vietnam War.

explicit_text: I was stationed twice at Utapao Royal Thai Navy Airfield in Thailand during the height of the Vietnam War. At its peak, there were about 12,000 U.S. GIs assigned there, and the base was a critical hub for supporting combat operations in Southeast Asia. After the war ended in 1975, I returned to Utapao when only about 300 personnel remained, working on recovering aircraft. The base was a busy and important location for the Air Force's mission during and after the war.

entity: Korat Royal Thai Air Force Base
explicit_text: I was temporarily assigned to Korat Royal Thai Air Force Base in Thailand for about 60 days, where I ran a shift crew despite being an airman first class. At Korat, we worked on repairing C-130 aircraft that had taken damage from combat missions, often landing on unapproved runways and under fire. The conditions were tough, with soft-sided hooches for housing and long work hours, but the work was critical to keeping the planes flying. Korat was one of the forward bases where I gained leadership experience and faced the realities of war support.
